title: Library Dust-Up
desc: The archive scrolls are reshelving themselves. Incorrectly.

steps.text:
The Great Archive of Kiru City has a self-sorting enchantment. Scrolls, once read, float gently back to their correct shelf. It's been working perfectly for two hundred years.

Today it's sorting by color.

Military records are filed with poetry. Tax rolls are shelved next to lullabies. A treatise on bridge construction has been wedged between two romance novels because they're all the same shade of beige.

The head archivist is standing in the middle of the chaos, eye twitching. "The resonance glyph in the sorting chamber has been vandalized. Someone etched a rude word into it. Please. Help me re-sort before the council session."

options.label: Try to fix the sorting glyph directly

steps.text:
Military records are easy to identify — they're heavier, sealed with wax, and smell faintly of iron. You stack them by date, checking seals. The scrolls resist your handling, tugging gently toward shelves where they don't belong. One keeps drifting toward the cooking section.

You wrestle it back three times before pinning it under a bookend. "Stay."

It takes two hours to re-sort the military section alone. The archivist is still working on history when you finish. She looks at your neat stacks and almost smiles.

"Thank you. Now I just need someone to fix the glyph before tomorrow, or they'll all go right back to sorting by color." She sighs. "At least it's not sorting by smell. That happened once. We don't talk about it."

options.label: Leave before you hear about the smell incident

steps.text:
The sorting chamber is in the archive basement — a small stone room with a crystal glyph embedded in the floor. The glyph is a complex web of lines and curves, each one representing a category. Sure enough, someone has scratched a crude word across the intersection of "subject" and "origin."

You have no idea how to repair a resonance glyph. But you do have a piece of chalk and a steady hand. You trace the original lines as best you can, filling in the vandalized section.

The glyph flickers. The archive above rumbles. Scrolls begin drifting — slowly at first, then faster — back to their correct shelves. Not perfect. History and philosophy are still mixed up. But military records are in the military section, and that's what matters.

The archivist appears at the basement door. "You fixed it?"
"Mostly."
"Mostly is a miracle. I'll take it."
